behaviors.description: Required patient fields (name, date of birth, insurance ID) are validated before downstream processing; missing fields produce a clear error listing all missing items.
behaviors.justification: Pure input validation logic with no I/O or external calls. Tests field presence checking against a single input object. A mock of downstream services cannot hide this failure — the validation happens before any downstream calls. No real infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that validates the intake form rejects submissions with missing name, DOB, or insurance ID, returns a clear error message listing each missing field, and accepts submissions when all three fields are present.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test operates on input validation alone; no need for real services or databases.

behaviors.description: Date of birth validation rejects future dates, dates more than 150 years ago, and non-date strings.
behaviors.justification: Isolated date-validation function. No I/O, no external calls, no LLM. Testing boundary conditions (today's date, 150-year threshold, invalid formats) is a pure logic problem. Mocking downstream services won't catch validation bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that validates DOB field rejects future dates, dates older than 150 years, non-date strings (e.g., 'abc', '2025-13-45'), and accepts valid birthdates within valid range.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Validates only the date-parsing and boundary logic; no external dependencies.

behaviors.description: The insurance eligibility check sends the correct patient ID and plan code to the payer API and correctly interprets the 'eligible' vs 'not eligible' vs 'pending' response.
behaviors.justification: Real external API call is required. A pure in-process mock could hide real failures: API response format changes, unexpected field names, TLS errors, timeouts, or malformed JSON that mocks don't reproduce. Only a real API call (or network-level stub matching real format) catches these. The definition of System explicitly includes 'real HTTP, or real external API call'.
behaviors.test_description: System test that calls the real payer API (or stage/sandbox environment) with test patient ID and plan code, verifies the request includes correct patient data, and validates the system correctly interprets the API response as 'eligible', 'not eligible', or 'pending'.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real API endpoint is required; mocks would hide format-change failures and network-level errors.

behaviors.description: When the payer API is unreachable, the system marks eligibility as 'pending' and continues the intake rather than blocking.
behaviors.justification: Testing real API failure modes (timeout, connection refused, DNS failure, partial response). A mock HTTP library raising a generic exception tests happy-path error handling but misses real failure characteristics: hanging sockets, TLS errors, slow timeouts, or incomplete responses that only occur with real network calls. System-level test exposing real unreachability is required to catch edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: System test that simulates real payer API unreachability (e.g., timeout, connection refused) and verifies the system marks eligibility as 'pending', logs the failure appropriately, and continues processing the intake without blocking or crashing.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real or network-stub API failure is required; in-process mocks hide real timeout and network-error behavior.

behaviors.description: The AI symptom triage correctly assigns high urgency to symptom combinations that indicate emergency conditions (e.g., chest pain + shortness of breath).
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real LLM medical reasoning. A mock AI engine returning fixed {urgency: 'high'} would always pass a test, but the real engine's classification is non-deterministic and may vary slightly on different runs. Running this test 100 times with the real LLM would produce different confidence scores or occasionally different urgency levels. Only the real AI/ML model call can verify the medical assessment is sound. Mocks cannot validate clinically correct reasoning.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test that submits a patient intake with reported chest pain and shortness of breath to the real symptom triage AI engine, runs this test multiple times to observe variation in confidence scores, and verifies that 'high urgency' is consistently assigned (or assigned with high confidence) for this symptom combination.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real LLM is required; mocks hide the non-determinism and would always pass regardless of model quality.

behaviors.description: The symptom triage produces a natural language explanation of its urgency assessment that a clinician can review, not just a numeric score.
behaviors.justification: Quality and existence of the explanation depends entirely on the real LLM. A mock could return a fixed explanation like 'Emergency symptoms detected', but the real LLM may produce verbose, unclear, or medically incoherent explanations that a clinician cannot act upon. Testing whether the explanation is clinically coherent and actionable requires the real LLM. Mocks cannot validate explanation quality or medical appropriateness.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test that calls the real symptom triage AI with various symptom combinations (routine, moderate, emergency), verifies each produces a non-empty natural language explanation (not just a numeric score), and spot-checks that explanations are coherent and reference the reported symptoms.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real LLM is required; explanation quality varies with model and cannot be validated by mocks.

behaviors.description: An appointment is scheduled in the first available slot that matches the assigned urgency level — high urgency gets same-day, medium gets within 48 hours.
behaviors.justification: Tests the scheduling logic against a real calendar database (local, shared). A pure mock of the database would always return slots in expected format, hiding real issues: database returns slots in unexpected format, slots overlap, or availability calculation is wrong. The real shared calendar is needed to verify slot matching works correctly. No external API calls or LLM needed — local database suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that populates a test calendar database with availability (same-day slots, 48-hour slots, beyond 48 hours), calls the scheduling step for a high-urgency patient, and verifies an appointment is scheduled in the first available same-day slot matching the urgency level.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real local database (calendar) is required; mocks would hide slot-matching and overlap issues.

behaviors.description: The scheduling step does not double-book a time slot even when two patients are being processed concurrently.
behaviors.justification: Tests database-level concurrency control and transaction isolation. A pure in-process mock is single-threaded and cannot expose race conditions. Real concurrent reads and writes to a database can reveal failures in locking or isolation level. A local test database with proper ACID properties can catch this via transaction isolation. OS-level synchronization (mutexes, file locking) may also be needed for full coverage (System level), but database-level transactions (Integration) catch most real double-booking failures.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that runs two concurrent patient intake processes targeting the same narrow availability window (e.g., the last available same-day slot), verifies that only one patient gets the slot while the other is assigned a later time, and checks logs to confirm no double-booking or scheduling conflict occurred.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real concurrent database access is required; mocks hide race conditions entirely.

behaviors.description: The confirmation message includes the patient name, appointment time, provider name, and preparation instructions appropriate to the visit type.
behaviors.justification: Tests message generation logic with data retrieved from a local database (patient info, appointment details, visit-type-specific instructions). A pure mock returning fixed strings would always pass. Real data lookup failures (wrong provider name, missing preparation instructions, visit-type mismatch) are caught only by reading from a real (test) database. No external API or LLM needed — local database queries suffice.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that completes a full intake for a test patient with a specific appointment time and visit type (e.g., cardiology consultation), then verifies the confirmation message includes patient name, appointment time, provider name, and visit-appropriate preparation instructions (e.g., 'Bring insurance card and recent test results').
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real database lookups are required; mocks would hide data-retrieval and lookup-mismatch failures.

behaviors.description: The full intake pipeline — validate, check insurance, triage symptoms, schedule, confirm — completes for a real patient submission without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: End-to-end test of the full workflow. The pipeline includes multiple infrastructure levels: Unit (validation), System (insurance API), Agentic (symptom triage LLM), and Integration (calendar DB, confirmation message). The bottleneck is the symptom triage: it is non-deterministic (LLM output varies across runs), cannot be reliably mocked, and is critical to the pipeline's correctness. Testing the full pipeline requires the real LLM triage step, making Agentic the minimum level. Workflow is also defensible if the emphasis is on coordinated multi-step execution.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test that submits a complete patient intake with valid data (including specific reported symptoms), integrates with the real triage LLM, the real/stage insurance API, and a test calendar database, and verifies the entire pipeline completes without manual intervention, producing a valid appointment and confirmation message.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real LLM triage is the limiting factor; this is the step that cannot be reliably mocked and is most likely to fail unpredictably.
